The '''Ocelot 1''' is a main battle tank and assault gun designed and developed by The System Offensive Mechanics in 1979 and produced in 1983. Developed in an era where entity onslaughts and armed conflicts saw a significant increase, the Ocelot's design focused on having both the capability to effectively deal with smaller targets, waves, fortifications, and armored vehicles, which have become increasingly common throughout the years. Since its initial production in [[The Warzone]], it has served as a vital resource exported and operated by the Republic of Aurielle's armed forces. Original plans for the Ocelot 1 were heavily inspired by the mechanical aspects of the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T-72 T-72's] design. The design for the Ocelot was developed from the failed Project 71 that intended to replace the dated ZIS-5 anti-aircraft mounts. There are four operational Ocelot versions: the '''1A1M''', '''1A2-J''', '''1A2-S''', and '''1A3''', though older variations are kept as emergency units. Unlike many [[The Frontrooms|Frontroom]] tanks, the Ocelot does not have a common armament among all its different variations, ranging from a smoothbore 125mm cannon to 4 23x115mm KwK automatic belt-fed cannons. These are operated by the SOM Ohotnik 3 fire control system, laser rangefinders, and a The System-licensed Auriellen day-night thermal imaging camera. The tank is powered by a V12 SE1 dual water-cooled diesel engines. The Ocelot 1 entered service in 1983, where it was deployed to assist forces in The Warzone; from then on, it has participated in various other conflicts, mainly by Aurielle. The Ocelot is currently only operated by The System and the Republic of Aurielle; however, various other tank designs, mainly the Cascadan YQ-9 Lǎong, are inspired by it. ==Development== The Ocelot project began initial development in March 1979 in order to develop a tank, the ''Arvan Standard Tank'' (AST), to replace the military's obsolete Soviet ZIS-5 anti-aircraft mounts, which, although abundant, would not suffice for a professional army. The detailed specifications for the tank were released the next month, and The System's government issued the order to The System Offensive Mechanics (SOM) to embark on the project. Under 'Project 71' (Проект 71; Projekt 71), three subsequent models were submitted, each following different doctrines: the XP-1, XP-2, and the XP-3. Cooperation between SOM members was rocky at the beginning, with many cultural differences and disagreements about the general appearance of the tank hampering progress. Some favored a tank optimized for utility and usefulness against groups, while others attached importance to maintaining production costs low. The XP-1, designed by Ove Arvidsson, followed the traditional Western tank doctrine, incorporating a large chassis with a manual loader. The XP-3 was a mix of early British and late-war German designs modernized, designed to have a stout hull with sloped armor. Ultimately, the small, sloped chassis design of the XP-2, Aledskii Gercho's design, was chosen due to its inspiration from Soviet designs and the heavy 105mm SA2 inspired by Western tanks. Later on, a flamethrower was added as concerns about the tank's ability to destroy levels mounted. During the initial design period, prototypes of the XP-2 and XP-1 were delivered, producing the XPA1 and XPA2. The testing immediately ruled out the XPA-1's design due to it being too large to fit in most indoor levels, thus rendering it only able to be used in outdoor far-reach levels, issues with weight, and fuel efficiency. Following the conceptual inception, the SOM began construction of a 400 mm thick cast steel armor chassis fitted with slat armor, with General Markus Utkin being placed in command of the design. This armor and chassis were designed to withstand the commonly used anti-tank rifles, high-explosive rounds, and shaped charges. As per requirements, they were fitted with Dual 700 HP V10 SE1 water-cooled engines to help traverse the fields of outdoor levels and to have enough power to push through walls on indoor ones. S2-M periscopes were equipped to the prototypes. During testing, mechanical errors plagued the engines, causing them to lose power when they lurched into craters abruptly; another issue was the engine refusing to start up, usually after hours of usage. Fixes went underway but were cut short due to the program being briefly cut in March of 1980 as material costs began to increase and the production facility was lost to a fire. Development resumed in December of 1980 as a new facility was built in the Warzone. The XPA1 and XPA2 were relocated to Cevasburg for further development. The XPA2 model still suffered major mechanical failures due to its faulty engine, which was eventually replaced by the newer V12 SE1 engine. Mobility issues were also of great concern, as the tank often sank under its weight into the mud, thus SOM developed the Schlammreifen (mud tires), multi-layer plated rubber tracks in February of 1981 to resolve the issues with mobility. The models with the Schlammreifen were also tested in [[Level 107]]'s snowfields, which showed no hindrance in performance under snowy conditions. Following the eventual selection of Gercho's XPA2 over the XPA1, three more XPA2s were produced, though they faced quality issues with their engines and transmissions. The engines would ingest flung debris and dust, thus breaking down periodically as well as causing the transmissions to become sticky and occasionally break. Covers were installed to help prevent debris intake while maintaining proper ventilation and air intake to the engine compartment. A few weeks later, a new turret designed by the North Arvan Ostberg Industries (NAOI) replaced the old turret, saving 1.5 tonnes in weight. In 1982, SOM went back to the drawing board to continue refining the XPA2's design. Improved transmission and suspension systems allowed for the tank to pass over trenches and craters without much difficulty. Crew testing the XPA2 complained about the difficulty of utilizing the fire control system and accurately calculating range. The fire control system was then simplified by replacing the TAS-10 with the Ohotnik-1. A stabilizer was also added later due to issues with accuracy during movement in uneven terrain. The prototype models remain in service as of 2025 as mobile last-defence units in Neuängles (Новёглсь). ==Production and improvement== Low-rate initial production (LRIP) of the tank was approved in early 1983, though wider production quickly began in May 1983 at the Cevasburg facility following some changes in the ballistic protection of the turret and changes in the engine and transmissions. 42 Ocelot A1s were initially ordered but were changed to 82 after an influx of steel production in Ulitsburg. In August of 1983, the first 23 completed Ocelots were combat tested in the front lines of Georgis. Based on experiences in Georgis, numerous changes were made for the following produced models. Production issues in the Cevasburg facility led to limited delivery of roughly 30 Ocelots total in January 1984. After 12, production of the Ocelot and its subsequent versions was produced in [[Level 618]], following the System's alliance with the Republic of Aurielle and later on Autonomous Colony of Geminia (Auriellen government in exile). Ocelots are produced and delivered in production batches; the first occurred in May 1983, the second in 1987, and the third in 1991. The currently earliest batch as of 2025 has been the 2019 delivery. The SOM Ohotnik-1 fire control system created many production delays as well, as the computer itself had a lengthy construction process. ===Further improvements=== While previous models only varied in detail, the Ocelot 1A2 was the first major improvement to the Ocelot, introducing the 125mm SA5 KwK and a better stabilizer. Starting with a sub-batch of some 5 vehicles, new tanks were fitted with new sideskirts and armor. Based on observations from the Lustruger Crescen War, The System set about a new program for potential combat improvements. Official military requirements were published later on December 1989. The program consisted of two stages, the first being mainly in regards to crew survivability and armor protection and the second being in regards to mobility and speed. The first stage replaced the old turret with a new one under the Ocelot 1A-2N, which had better ballistic protection and included a new autoloader that deviated from the traditional design to be compatible with blow-out panels and the ammo being stored separately in armored compartments, significantly reducing weight and increasing crew survivability, though this came with some issues as rough terrain somewhat slowed or jammed the process. Stage 2 of the program focused on removing or simplifying unnecessary parts while not jeopardizing quality. The exact parts removed or changed have yet to be specified, though production costs and the general weight of the vehicle decreased. The Ocelot 1A1M upgraded the engine, added an advanced ERA package, and upgraded the mounted flamethrower, while the Ocelot 1A2-J added a commander-controlled ATGM and a new machine gun. The latest version of the tank is the Ocelot 1S, modernized Auriellen-licensed versions of the Ocelot, which entered service in an initial batch of five tanks in 2009, including a domestically produced stabilizer, improved thermal imaging, composite armor, hardened 125mm SA5, and other modernizations. The Ocelot has not been exported out to any other group but smaller militant factions have begun attempting to copy the Ocelot design features but have produced nothing relatively comparable. ==Design== ===Protection=== The Ocelot, depending on model, one-to-three tiers of protection. The first tier is composite armor in the turret, composed of rolled steel plating, with an insert of alternating layers of aluminum and plastics and a controlled deformation section; flat armor surrounds the hull, backside of the turret and engine compartment. The second tier is the Zanaves-3 explosive reactive armor (ERA) bricks, which degrade the penetrating power of explosive projectiles; this is only present within certain variations of the tank. The third tier is a Rajah Siretea di Vicinus Integratem-1integrated proximity system (SVI-1) Active Protection System, found exclusively in the Ocelot 1A2-S, produced by Allet Softworks. The system constantly tracks and identifies potential threats via laser warning receivers and thermal identification; when the tank has been 'painted' by a weapon-guidance laser, the SVT-1 will warn the tank crew. Upon the detection of an incoming projectile, a soft-kill smoke grenade obscurant will be discharged over a general area (precisiveness is unfeasible due to technological limitations, but it will calculate the approach vector, the nature of the threat, the approximate time to impact, and a rough angle of approach). If the threat is not neutralized, a [[Object 15|Firesalt]]-based high explosive fragmentation countermeasure is deployed over the general area of impact; even if it passes, that cluster-munition-like fragmentation countermeasure will be distributed as a last-ditch effort to stop it. The armor is most substantial at the front, though the side and the rear of the tank have protection against heavy machine guns, medium-caliber rounds, and other types of explosives such as [[Object 15|Firesalt]]. The side of the hull is covered by armor skirts to increase protection against projectiles and rocket-propelled grenades. As mines become an increasing concern during conflict, the hull floor's sides were sloped 45°.
[[File:Serval ready to ambush.png|link=]] An Ocelot 1A2-S camouflaging in [[Level 427]] during Operation Hora Vainal.final hour
Aside from APS and armor, in most newer variants, the ammunition and autoloader are located in compartments separated from the crew. In case of a detonation, the blow-off panels on the compartment roofs will direct the explosion and fire away from the crew. The Ocelot 1A2-S replaced the traditional ERA with a wedge-shaped armor module; however, it came at the cost of increasing its weight. Newer Ocelots have protection against nuclear, biological, and chemical (NBC) threats through an overpressurization system; they are also protected from unstable levels as newer Ocelots have [[Architect Industries]]-made reality stabilization modules. ===Armament=== The main armament for most Ocelots is either the SOM 105mm or SOM 125mm smoothbore gun. The gun is capable of firing HEAT, HE and HESH rounds. It has an effective range of around 100 m to 5.5 km and is fully stabilized. As part of its armament the tank includes two-to-three 7.92 modified MG-34/42 machine guns, with the Ocelot 1A2-J changing the commander's machine gun was replaced with an internally controlled ATGM, and the secondary gunner's machine gun was replaced with a 20x138mm MG-23 heavy machine gun with 1700 rounds available. The Ocelot A1, 1A1M, and A2 use a turret mounted FKwK FSA3 [[Object 5|Greek Fire]] flamethrower. The SOM 105mm and SOM 125mm are fed by an automated loader and a manual loader, depending on the model. The autoloader is similar to that of the [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Leclerc_tank Frontroom's Leclerc] (according to no-clipped French tankers), consisting of a continuous conveyor belt magazine made of 22 cells and a rammer assembly. Up to five different types of ammunition can be selected, but currently only three are available. The cell positions the selected round for loading and disengages the main gun from the stabilization process and locks it in. Then, a rammer pushes the round from the cell into the gun breech, and when finished loading, the weapon is automatically driven back to the specified angle in accordance with the fire control system. In case of failure, ammunition may be manually loaded in, and the tank commander acts as an ad hoc loader. In the Ocelot 1A3 the main armament was replaced with four 23x115mm KwK automatic belt-fed canons and a Vbitsya-1 automatic thermal locking lead system. ===Mobility=== The Ocelot has dual 700 HP V12 SE1 water-cooled diesel engines. Different models of the Ocelot, mainly those produced after the Ocelot 1A-2N, are fitted with an upgraded V16 SE3 engine or dual 850 HP V16 SE5 engine, which has 800 HP and 850 HP respectively. The V12 SE1 engines can attain a top speed of 60 km/h on even terrain and up to 45 km/h on rough terrain. The tank has an air conditioning system for work in hot places. Suspension is done through torsion bars. ==Sourcing== Level 618, where Ocelots are assembled, is the main source of materials for the Ocelot's armor, as four factories produce various metals and plastics of unknown origin. In terms of armament, engine, and various mechanical aspects of the Ocelot, they are sourced from the levels within the War's System and connecting levels. Much like the Seborna, the upgraded cameras are made from the eyes of Counter Entities, with upgrades or additions through objects such as the [[Object 26|Moonlantern]] and [[Object 25|Flashies]]. ERA blocks are made from a simple design in which a thin line of Firesalt faces outwards to lessen the impact of projecties; it is protected from activation by small arms through Kevlar slabs. More sophisticated designs usually fix sensitivity issues. The Ocelot 1A2-S' wedge-shaped spaced multilayer composite armor is sourced from [[Level 15]]. While the autoloader's conveyor belt is obtained from repurposing the conveyor belt in unsalvageable facilities in Level 618 or The Warzone, which appear to regenerate every so often, or from other levels with machines; additional mechnical parts of the autoloader are either copied from Level 15 or [[Level 754]]. Ammunition is made from both Firesalt and [[Object 10|Backshrooms]], and any other offensive material that can be used. ==Variants== ===Ocelot 1A-1 1983-1984=== The Ocelot 1A-1 had minor mechanical upgrades to its engine and turret rotation mechanism, as well as the incorporation of an improved stabilizer. The 1A-1 integrated various comfort-of-life features, such as the simplification and repositioning a little forward of the driver panel and the substitution of the manual cranks for a joystick FCS-assisted controller. A spotlight was included in the model, which had the capability to be rotated 30 degrees on a motor by the tank commander; it was placed on the right side of the turret. Deliveries of the 1A-1 occured in 1983 and ended in 1984. 30 vehicles were delivered during its time. ===Ocelot 1A-2N 1985=== The Ocelot 1A-2N (N for Nacht, night) is the designation given to upgraded vehicles of a sub-batch of Ocelots 1As. This modernization replaced the old engine with the new 800 HP upgraded V16 SE3 engine, installed the SOM-NV-1RT dual night vision, and removed the spotlight. The 1A-2N also replaced the obsolete Ohotnik 1, which had difficulty tracking and accurately hitting targets while the tank was moving above 40 km/h, with the SOM Ohotnik 3 fire control system. 15 vehicles were delivered in 1985. A new turret with better ballistic protection and an included new autoloader was also introduced; following the development of the C24N Seborna and its subsequent variant, the C24N9SEP Mobile Armored Platform incorporated a modified Ocelot 1A-2N turret. The 1A-2N's systems and technologies became standard among the following Ocelot models developed. ===Ocelot 1A1M - 2003-present=== Upgraded version of old Ocelots. The main feature is the inclusion of advanced ERA armor packages, an upgraded FKwK FSA3 flamethrower with additional range and fuel capacity, and an upgraded dual 850 HP V16 SE5 engine, seen in newer models. The shells compatible with the old loading system were upgraded, and a new fragmentation HE shell for the 1A1M was introduced. ===Ocelot 1A2 - 1987-2001=== The 1A2 includes the most substantial changes, is a major development technologically. The Ocelot 1A2 is a modernized A1 version, including a rudimentary armor package with explosive reactive armor (ERA), and a new 125mm SA5 KwK, replacing the 105mm. The FCS was replaced with the much more precise Ohotnik 6 system, as well as the stabiliser was upgraded to the Sure-Fire gyroscopic inertial dual-axis system. This version was sent to Aurielle for inspection and testing for export as their main battle tank. A fuel injector was added to the engine to produce smoke screens. A new autoloading system replaced the loader, allowing for ammo to be quickly reloaded and reducing the crew from 4 to 3. This system allowed the tank to carry more ammunition in its hull while maintaining protection. ===Ocelot 1A2-J 2004-present=== The Ocelot 1A2-J (J for Jagd, hunt) is a derivative of the Ocelot 1A2 and the most widespread version for both Aurielle and The System. Made in response to wider geopolitical tensions and rapidly militarizing organizations, it upgrades the thermal imaging camera with the Auriellen, Servon Dynamics-produced ANONF-99 day-night thermal imaging camera, which, aside from its thermal, has the ability to switch to a [[Object 25|Flashies]] lens; replaces the commander's machine gun with an internally controlled ATGM; and replaces the gunner's secondary machine gun with a 20x138mm MG-23 heavy machine gun that has 1700 rounds. ===Ocelot 1A2-S 2009-present=== Ocelot 1A2-S (S for Serval) is an Auriellen Ground Forces tank based on the Ocelot 1A2-J. 27 units have been built, all of which are license-produced in Aurielle. The tank features increased armor on the turret (including a wedge design) and hull; thus making it the heaviest tank of the Ocelot 1 family, improved stabilization, and control- and fire-control systems. It has additional ERA package kits if extra protection is necessary. ===Ocelot 1A3 2008-present=== The Ocelot 1A3 is a self-propelled anti-aircraft gun with a high fire rate platform based on the Ocelot chassis. Intended for both anti-aircraft roles and to quickly dispatch entity threats while maintaining the cover of an Ocelot. The main armament was removed and replaced with 4 23x115mm KwK automatic belt-fed cannons, with the turret being raised and given a quick horizontal and vertical traverse. The Ohotnik 6 FCS was kept, but it incorporated a new Vbitsya-1 automatic thermal locking lead system. ===Ocelot-Based Recovery Vehicle 2010-present=== The Ocelot-Based Recovery Vehicle (OBRV) is an armored recovery vehicle based on the Ocelot chassis. Certain modifications, such as the Assault Bridge kit, can allow it to function as a bridgelayer.
[[File:GhEeb0xWoAAUmyA.webp|link=]] Two Ocelot 1A3s performing live-fire exercises on the banks of [[The Chain]]
==The Warzone 1983-present== The Ocelot was immediately combat-tested in Georgis against entity hordes. Between the spring and fall of 1983, 12 Ocelot 1As were sent to The Warzone following reports of an uptick in entity raids. Assisting mechanized forces, the Ocelot was implemented to single-handedly defend positions while covering reinforcing troop emplacements. Its armor proved effective against entity threats and environmental hazards, with it being utilized against mines and unexploded ordnance in newly captured territory. Ocelots continue to be deployed in anti-entity campaigns around major settlements and regularly patrol highways between villages. On 12 June 2016, four Ocelots served a two-month tour on the Warzone, where they participated in a major offensive to capture territory; they were used to carve deep into entity territory, providing footholds for hive destructions. ==Outlier Skirmishes 1992-1996== In September 1992, twelve Ocelots were sent as part of the Göttrenk Contingent to defend supply routes and provide inter-level security against outlier groups. Two more vehicles of the Tank Company 15 were sent from Jazspe to Göttrenk; two weeks after their deployment, they were ambushed by a group of militants at a security checkpoint but were killed by returning fire. In February 1993, five Ocelots participated in a large anti-insurgency operation. On 26 June 1995, an Ocelot 1A2 fired five warning shots above the town of Trianska. In early 1996, most Ocelots finished their combat tour in Göttrenk and returned to their respective levels. ==Korthie Casiagen War 2000-2019== In 2000 the Autonomous Colony of Geminia had ten Ocelots at its disposal. Initial major offensives on UKOPCOM-occupied lands were led by Auriellen-operated Ocelots, which held significantly for longer periods of time, as neither MEG, Antlia, or the Confederation of Level 154 had the weaponry to counter them. In 2001, following Branch Auriga's creation and pressure to support Aurielle, several more Ocelots were gifted to Aurielle on March as part of The System aid package; they began to be trained by The System, and were later deployed in May against an offensive on MEG forces in Dracstor. Ocelots continued to be heavily used by Auriellen forces throughout 2002; on May 23, seven Ocelots captured the strategic city of Sapin alongside several infantry forces as part of Operation Thunderbolt. In late 2002, following the end of training of Auriellen forces by Aurigan personnel, The System ramped up its support and returned the Auriellen settlements in Level 618 (who had come under temporary occupation by The System to avoid MEG capture of the strategic location); over half of the next batch of Ocelots was purchased by or gifted to Aurielle. While new Auriellen Ocelot crews were being trained, a tank platoon participated in the Battle of Selumna. Throughout the following years, The System continued to support Aurielle, though deliveries of Ocelots were ceased as Aurielle began domestically producing them. As the Ocelot 1A1M and 1A2-J were introduced, the Auriellen government decided to return many of the old Ocelot models to be switched for their newer models or to be brought to the quality standards of the newer models. In January 2004, Auriellen tanks halted Antlian-Confederation of Level 154 advances near Lerna by providing direct fire in support of Aurigan and Cascadan Expeditionary Forcesname of Cascadan foreign volunteers participating in the Korthie Casiagen War (CEF) infantry forces. On January 30th, two Ocelot tank platoons participated in the Samacan offensive. The first casualty suffered by a crew operating an Ocelot happened in December 2006 when an Ocelot 1A2 was hit by a mine in Namaro, disabling movement. The location was then attacked by MEG personnel, leading to all but the driver dying as the vehicle exploded. Following the capture of Naramo, the vehicle was towed to Forward Operating Base Melania. The new Ocelot 1A3 vehicles were purchased by the Auriellen government due to a report of a major influx of troops being sent to Aurielle. They participated in countless bloody and brutal battles, gaining the nickname "Nurem's Falling Stars" from MEG soldiers. The following year, in 2009, the first Ocelot 1A2-S was made and later sent to assist the CEF troops on the Fokunuja offensive on Solevar. Ocelot tanks of different variants took extensive part in the Caranda Offensive, firing over 100 rounds in support of Auriellen-Aurigan advances as they recaptured Aurielle, annexed Antlia, and advanced into MEG-Confederation of Level 154 territory. ==Insurgency in The Chain (2007)== The System's military response to the Basechani insurgency included Ocelot tanks. Three Ocelots were seen engaging Basechani observation posts in the latter stages of the conflict. ==Lasta Vergos (2020)== The Lasta Vergos marked the first post-Korthie Casiagen War deployment of Ocelots. As part of Operation Resolutio Mordeleusmordillan resolution in La Santa Corona, the 14th Armored Company of the 1st Combined Arms Brigade (CAB) was deployed alongside mechanized battalions for peacekeeping purposes. Following Aurielle's intervention on the side of the Republican Mordillo, Ocelots advanced alongside [[C24N Seborna]]s to capture Ojoscuridad and other large LSC cities. Ten Ocelots participated in the shelling of the LSC Parliamentary Building. ==[[Cascadan Conflict]] (2020)== The Ocelot saw extensive service in the Cascadan Conflict. Ocelot columns moved through Cascadan lands following Aurielle's intervention and arrived at the front lines shortly afterwards. The 6th Armored Company participated in the Battle of Sinyong, where the largest armored battle of the conflict occurred, followed by various Auriellen armored units launching an offensive to recapture Sinyong and Dargol. The 87th Armored Company advanced through a secondary road that led to surrounding areas and Minahoro and then attacked Rahung; the 87th Armored Company made a subsequent breakthrough in Zhaomen. A column of Ocelots of the 2nd CAB attacked Kusan and Xiaonhosu. Ocelots also assisted other forces in Sarigeup and Sunai. ===Additional Combat History=== {{Notice||body=''This list is '''incomplete'''; you can help by '''expanding it'''.''}} *1990-2021: Harisash crisis (Aurielle and The System) *2016-2021: Vrendan War (Aurielle) *2021: Second Insurgency in The Chain (The System) *2021: War in Bugon (Aurielle) *2021: Operation Tre Burrow (Aurielle) *2019-2021: Münneuve Campaign (The System) *2019-2021: Burdskan Insurgency (Aurielle) *2021: Kalasho Coup (Aurielle) *2022: Exploitation War (Aurielle and The System) *2022-2024: Sirilis-Davernan Conflict (Aurielle and The System) *2021-2024: Cascadan Emergency (Aurielle and The System) *2024: Niwerald-Kagazi Skirmishes (Aurielle and The System) *2024: Operation Noviscu Talon (Aurielle) *2024: Davernan War (Aurielle and The System) *2024-2025: Muves Front (The System) *2024-present: Yaf Campaign (The System) *2025: Alensir Conflict (Aurielle) ==Operators== *[[File:The System Flag.png|25px]] The System *[[File:Republic of Aurielle New.png|25px]] Republic of Aurielle ---- {{Footnotes}} ---- {{Collapse|num=238232|initial='''> Open Author and Licensing Credits'''|final='''> Close Author and Licensing Credits'''|body=
